questions.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and CSS Grid?
questions.answer: Flexbox is one-dimensional: it lays items along a row OR a column. Great for: navbars, button groups, centering, distributing items along one axis. Grid is two-dimensional: rows AND columns simultaneously. Great for: overall page layout, complex card grids, magazine-style layouts. Use Flexbox for UI components (nav, buttons, forms). Use Grid for page structure and complex two-dimensional layouts. They complement each other — a Grid cell can contain a Flexbox container.

questions.question: What does "mobile-first" mean in responsive design?
questions.answer: Mobile-first means writing CSS for mobile screens first, then using min-width media queries to add styles for larger screens. Example: .grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } /* mobile: 1 column */ @media (min-width: 768px) { .grid { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } /* tablet: 2 */ This is better than "desktop-first" (max-width queries) because: mobile users are majority (60%+), mobile CSS is simpler (often less code), progressive enhancement is good practice.

questions.question: What is the box model in CSS?
questions.answer: Every CSS element is a box with four layers (inside out): Content (the actual text/image), Padding (space inside the border), Border (the visible border), Margin (space outside the border). box-sizing: border-box (recommended) makes width and height include padding and border — more intuitive sizing. box-sizing: content-box (default) means width is just the content, so adding padding/border makes the element larger than expected. Always set * { box-sizing: border-box; } at the start of your CSS.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency CSS Core Principles Part 2A queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
